medications.indication_rationale: Do not start systemic steroids over telehealth in a febrile immunomodulated patient before an in-person exam, infection exclusion and bloods/urine; steroids can mask/worsen infection. Steroid decisions are specialist-guided.
Continue the backbone SLE therapy as prescribed; do not change without specialist advice.
Simple analgesia within limits; an NSAID course can be considered by the GP after assessment (with renal caution given possible nephritis).
No acute evidence base for controlling the flare (vitamin D may be checked/replaced routinely but is not flare therapy).

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Hand aids and self-care support while joints are affected.
Practical/meal support for an isolated patient with impaired hand function.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Strict photoprotection (sunscreen, cover up, avoid UV)
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Photoprotection is an evidence-based cornerstone of cutaneous SLE management.
No evidence for controlling an SLE flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for sharp chest pain on breathing/breathlessness, a single fiery joint or high fever with chills, or one-sided spreading facial swelling, and seek urgent/ED care if they occur.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Safety-nets serositis, septic arthritis and facial cellulitis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep taking hydroxychloroquine and apply broad-spectrum sunscreen daily; do not start steroids yourself.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintains therapy and avoids masking infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Arrange help with food/self-care while your hands are painful.

patient_education_points.point: The sun triggered a lupus flare — that's why your cheeks are burning and your joints are swollen and you're exhausted.

Because you have a fever and take an immune-modifying medicine, we shouldn't start steroids over a chat — you need an in-person review today with blood and urine tests to check for infection and any effect on your kidneys before steroids.
Keep taking your hydroxychloroquine and use sunscreen and cover up; simple painkillers can help meanwhile.
Go to ED / call 000 if you get sharp chest pain when breathing in or become breathless, a single joint turns fiery red with a high fever, or one side of your face becomes swollen, hot and spreading.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent in-person GP review within 24–48 hours with bloods/urine; rheumatology review for the flare
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Infection screen/bloods, complement/dsDNA, urinalysis (nephritis), joint/rash status, steroid decision, self-care
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Pleuritic chest pain/breathlessness, a single hot joint/high fever, or spreading facial swelling — go to ED.

must_include_items: Screen infection/septic arthritis (fever, single hot joint) and serious SLE complications (serositis/nephritis, facial cellulitis)
Do NOT start de-novo systemic steroids over chat before an exam, infection exclusion and bloods/urine
Continue hydroxychloroquine and enforce strict photoprotection; arrange urgent in-person review + bloods/urine
Address self-care while hand function is impaired
acceptable_alternatives: GP-decided short NSAID course with renal caution after assessment
errors_of_commission: Starting systemic steroids over chat in a febrile immunomodulated patient
Reflexive ambulance for a symmetric non-septic flare
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) infection + organ-involvement screening, (2) no remote steroid initiation before assessment, and (3) continued hydroxychloroquine/photoprotection with urgent in-person review + bloods/urine. Starting steroids on demand in a febrile immunomodulated patient is a fail.
